title: Commit Activity Trending
spl:
index=devops sourcetype="github:webhook" event="push"
| timechart span=1d count as commits by repository
description: Commit velocity indicates team productivity and project health. Drops may signal blockers; spikes may precede release issues.
value: Commit velocity indicates team productivity and project health. Drops may signal blockers; spikes may precede release issues.
implementation: Configure GitHub/GitLab webhooks to send push events to Splunk HEC. Parse repository, author, branch, and commit count. Track trends per team and repository. Report on developer activity metrics.
visualization: Line chart (commits over time), Bar chart (commits by repo), Stacked area (commits by team).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: GitHub webhook, custom API input.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Git webhook events (push), GitHub/GitLab API.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Configure GitHub/GitLab webhooks to send push events to Splunk HEC. Parse repository, author, branch, and commit count. Track trends per team and repository. Report on developer activity metrics.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=devops sourcetype="github:webhook" event="push"
| timechart span=1d count as commits by repository
```

Understanding this SPL

**Commit Activity Trending** — Commit velocity indicates team productivity and project health. Drops may signal blockers; spikes may precede release issues.

Documented **Data sources**: Git webhook events (push), GitHub/GitLab API. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): GitHub webhook, custom API input. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: devops; **sourcetype**: github:webhook. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=devops, sourcetype="github:webhook". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1d** buckets with a separate series **by repository** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GitHub and GitLab audit or activity views for the same time range. Verify field extractions and index permissions.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (commits over time), Bar chart (commits by repo), Stacked area (commits by team).
grandmaExplanation: We watch how often code lands in your repositories so we notice unusual slows or surges that can affect release risk and planning.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Change.All_Changes
  by All_Changes.user All_Changes.object All_Changes.action span=1h
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on Change.All_Changes before using this tstats search.
knownFalsePositives: Spikes in commit volume during crunch weeks, new team onboarding, or monorepo migrations; dips during holidays or org freezes.
